title: The Macro Minute: Is the US economy being run too hot, Pt. III?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, with our
macro minute for Monday, May 18th, 2026.
As always, we'll start with the
executive summary from today's lead-off
morning note. So, let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, is the US
economy being run too hot, part three?
Our answer is yes, from the perspective
of the monetary policy and liquidity
cycles, according to key leading
indicators of nominal GDP growth from
the month of May. While our macro meta
model currently views the momentum of
these two key macro cycles as tailwinds
for risk assets, a likely hawkish pivot
by the Fed risks turning the monetary
policy and liquidity cycles into
explicit headwinds for risk assets in
the coming months. This outcome would
likely be digested poorly by market
participants who remain overwhelmingly
bullish and keen to crowd into one
theme, which is obviously AI. Transition
to our 42 macro dashboard, as always,
we'll wrap up with a question from our
community. This one's titled low chance
of a correction. Says, "Hi skipper,
thanks for another great around the horn
presentation today. As always, a lot to
learn and ponder. Where I'm struggling
to connect the dots is the low risk of a
correction in the short to medium term
signaled by the positioning model. Can
you please elaborate on why this might
be, but the tech semi seems so crowded?
I believe it was just yesterday that you
mentioned the macro minute that we may
be witnessing the start of a correction
now, which the low risk reading on the
positioning model seems to clash with.
Is it perhaps the positioning model's
already picked up on a down move having
begun and now signaling low risk of a
correction after the one that may be
already underway, so to speak, or is it
the wrong way to look at it? I'd love to
hear you expand on this. Have a great
week." So, appreciate you. Uh, thanks
for the kind feedback. Uh, thank you for
the thoughtful question as well. Uh, so,
the first thing I will say is, uh, two
things. One,
the positioning cycle itself is not a
catalyst for the momentum and dispersion
within and across asset markets. It is
an accelerant to the momentum and
dispersion within and across asset
markets to the extent you have positive
or negative surprises that are either
transitory or trend, uh, in from the
perspective of the other five key macro
cycles being growth, inflation, monetary
policy, fiscal policy, and liquidity.
So, when you think about a correction,
what causes corrections is the evidence
of, you know, some some degree of
crowded bull positioning, either high
or, you know, medium moderate degree of
crowded bull positioning, and then you
have a transitory negative surprise in
one or more of those other five key
cycles. What causes a market crash is if
you have a high degree of crowded bull
positioning, and you have a trending
negative surprise in one or more of
those other key uh macro cycles. And so,
right now, what we're essentially
calling for is, hey, we had a uh decent
amount of crowded bull positioning uh
from a broad risk perspective, not
necessarily, you know, the AI trade.
Obviously, the AI trade has a tremendous
amount of crowded bull positioning, uh
but it from a broad market risk
perspective, uh there was a uh
moderate risk uh reasonable risk of
crowded bull positioning, which is a 50
to 75% chance of a correction,
specifically according to the
positioning model. But then now, we're
layering on these sort of transitory
negative surprises from the perspective
of uh the the inflation cycle, from the
perspective of the liquidity cycle, and
and and the monetary policy cycle. So,
those negative surprises, relative to
the expectations of market participants
who built up that crowded bull
positioning, will cause them to change
their positioning in a way that makes
asset prices uh go down. And so, that is
the risk. And so, you have to remember
when you're interpreting the positioning
model, uh the second part of of the
question, second answer to the question,
is when you're interpreting the
positioning model, the positioning model
in and of itself, you know, it has
legacy, has memory. So, uh the most
recent update of the positioning model
took the the risk of a correction down
because one of the indicators uh
exhibited a pullback of that crowded
bull positioning. But again, this is a
as a function of the unwind of the
beginning of the unwind of that crowded
bull positioning that we saw last week.
So, you know, there is still a legacy of
having that crowded bull positioning,
and there's also uh the the risk of
these, you know, the monetary policy
cycle
and the uh liquidity cycle inflecting
into negative trends or inflecting into
a headwind condition, much like what we
see with the inflation cycle. And so if
that continues, then the starting point
of having a crowded degree of bullish
positioning irrespective of what is
currently signaling today, the starting
point of what we had at the beginning of
last week from a positioning model
standpoint is the is the risk in the
context of those negative developments
in the other five key macro cycles. So
we'll wrap it up there. Darius Dale here
presenting our macro minute from Monday,
May 18th, 2026. Best of luck out there
today. We'll catch back here tomorrow.
Cheers.
